title: Dermatoscopic Image Classification via ConvNeXt and Transfer Learning for Melanoma Screening
research_question: 1. Why did you choose to work on classification for automated skin lesion diagnosis, particularly using advanced deep learning architectures combined with comprehensive image preprocessing? 2. Why did you choose to focus on pre-trained models and minority class image synthesis methods rather than alternative approaches to improve generalisation and reliability across diverse lesion types?
research_objectives: To analyse and evaluate existing deep learning-based skin lesion classification techniques, identifying their limitations and potential improvements using state-of-the-art architectures such as InceptionV3, EfficientNetB0, Xception, and ConvNeXt.

To enhance the quality of dermoscopic images through effective preprocessing techniques, including digital hair removal using the DullRazor algorithm, colour normalisation, and white balancing, in order to improve classification performance.

To mitigate class imbalance within skin lesion datasets by applying data augmentation, oversampling, and synthetic image generation methods to improve minority class representation and model robustness.

To design, evaluate, and deploy an automated skin lesion classification system, leveraging pre-trained deep learning models for improved generalisation and efficiency, and implementing a web-based application to demonstrate practical performance and validation metrics.
data_details: The HAM10000 ('Human Against Machine with 10000 training images') dataset consists of 10,015 dermatoscopic images collected from different populations, acquired and stored by different modalities. Cases include seven diagnostic categories: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease (akiec), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), benign keratosis-like lesions (bkl), dermatofibroma (df), melanoma (mel), melanocytic nevi (nv), and vascular lesions (vasc). Ground truth is confirmed through histopathology (>50%), follow-up examination, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy. Images are 600×450 pixels saved in JPEG format. The dataset is highly class-imbalanced; nv is the dominant class (6,705 images) while df is the smallest (115 images). The dataset is sourced from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/kmader/skin-cancer-mnist-ham10000).
literature_review_summary: **Topic Overview:** Automated dermatoscopic image classification for melanoma screening is a critical challenge in medical AI, where variability in lesion appearance, lighting, and acquisition settings creates significant domain shift. Large benchmark datasets such as HAM10000 and SIIM-ISIC challenge corpora have advanced the field but expose persistent class imbalance and patient-level data leakage issues (Cassidy, 2022; Kurtansky et al., 2024). **Importance of the Field:** Melanoma is among the deadliest skin cancers when detected late, yet dermoscopy-based triage can improve early detection rates substantially. Automated classification reduces clinician burden and enables screening in underserved areas. Computational tools evaluated on histopathology-confirmed test sets are increasingly recognised as viable clinical decision-support instruments (Naqvi et al., 2023; Ray et al., 2024). **Key Technical Content:** Preprocessing is a core pipeline component. DullRazor remains widely used for hair removal (Kasmi et al., 2023), while deep learning-based inpainting offers superior quality at higher computational cost (Delibasis et al., 2023). GAN-based colour normalisation (DermoCC-GAN) improved multiclass accuracy by several percentage points (Branciforti et al., 2023). CLAHE and white-balancing reduce inter-device variance (Salvi et al., 2024). Architecturally, ConvNeXt (Liu et al., 2022) achieved ImageNet top-1 ~87.8%, matching transformers in natural image tasks and demonstrating strong transferability to dermatoscopic classification when fine-tuned with class-balanced sampling (Ray et al., 2024; Himel et al., 2024). Ensemble strategies combining DenseNet121, Xception, and EfficientNetB0 achieved AUC ~0.96 on curated splits (Naqvi et al., 2023). Transformer and Swin-based hybrids capture long-range context but offer marginal gains over carefully tuned CNNs at higher compute cost (Gulzar et al., 2022; Sarker, 2022). **Findings and Insights:** Transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained weights with gradual unfreezing consistently outperforms training from scratch. Clinical covariates (age, lesion site) fused with image features improve melanoma AUC (Himel et al., 2024). Patient-level splits yield lower but more realistic metrics than image-level cross-validation (Cassidy, 2022). Sensitivity for melanoma at a fixed false-positive rate is the clinically meaningful evaluation criterion (Ray et al., 2024). **Limitations Identified:** Dataset bias toward lighter skin tones, inconsistent annotation quality, and absence of prospective clinical validation limit translation. Image-level evaluation inflates reported performance due to patient-level leakage (Cassidy, 2022). Preprocessing pipelines are rarely standardised across studies. **Unresolved Issues:** Generalisation across datasets and acquisition devices remains unproven. Fairness across skin tones is unaddressed. Interpretability and model calibration for clinical deployment are insufficiently studied. ConvNeXt's behaviour under different fine-tuning strategies in dermatoscopy remains under-explored (Ray et al., 2024).
research_gap: Despite considerable advances in automated skin lesion classification, several critical methodological gaps persist in the existing literature. First, the majority of high-performing models rely on singular preprocessing steps — either hair removal or colour normalisation — applied in isolation, without systematic evaluation of their combined and sequential impact on deep learning model performance. There is no established benchmark for an integrated preprocessing pipeline that incorporates DullRazor-based hair removal, channel-level colour normalisation, and contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalisation (CLAHE) applied together before model training. Second, while ConvNeXt has demonstrated strong results on natural image benchmarks, comparative evaluation of ConvNeXt-Tiny against multiple established architectures — InceptionV3, EfficientNetB0, and Xception — under identical training protocols, identical preprocessing pipelines, and lesion-level data splits remains absent in the dermatoscopy literature. Existing studies provide only preliminary pairwise comparisons, leaving systematic multi-model benchmarking on HAM10000 unexplored. Third, class imbalance in HAM10000 is widely acknowledged, yet weighted sampling strategies combined with lesion-ID-based train-validation-test splits that prevent patient-level data leakage are rarely implemented simultaneously. Most works either apply augmentation alone or use image-level splits, both of which produce overly optimistic estimates. Fourth, the deployment gap — bridging a trained classification model to a clinical-facing web application that provides per-class probability outputs and risk-stratified melanoma alerts — remains unaddressed. Existing research stops at offline evaluation, with no practical triage interface validated against dermatoscopic images. Closing these gaps through a unified pipeline integrating standardised multi-step preprocessing, comparative transfer learning, lesion-level evaluation, and web-based deployment represents a timely and necessary contribution to the field.
base_paper_summary: **Paper:** 'Enhanced Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Optimized CNN Architecture and Checkpoints for Automated Dermatological Lesion Classification' (BMC Medical Imaging, 2024). **Pipeline:** The research employs a comprehensive pipeline starting with Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) on the HAM10000 dataset. Preprocessing involves image resizing, pixel normalisation, and class-imbalance mitigation through targeted data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming). A custom CNN architecture with multiple convolutional, pooling, and dense layers is designed specifically for seven-class skin lesion classification. Model checkpointing is used to save best-performing weights during training, and training-validation splits are applied to monitor overfitting. **Dataset:** HAM10000 — 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven classes: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, and vasc, with confirmed ground truth via histopathology, expert consensus, or confocal microscopy. Class imbalance is addressed through augmentation. **Algorithm Accuracy:** The study compares multiple CNN configurations. Traditional ML classifiers (SVM, KNN, Random Forest) report accuracies in the 55–72% range on lesion features. The custom CNN without augmentation achieves approximately 71%. The optimised CNN with augmentation and checkpointing achieves the best accuracy of approximately 74.2%. **Best Algorithm:** The optimized CNN with data augmentation and checkpoint-based model selection achieves the highest accuracy at approximately 74.2%, outperforming baseline CNNs and classical ML approaches on the seven-class classification task. **Novel Element:** The novelty lies in combining an application-specific custom CNN architecture with a systematic checkpointing strategy that preserves the best validation weights, paired with targeted augmentation addressing class imbalance without external generative models. **Future Recommendations:** The paper recommends integration of transformer-based or attention-enhanced architectures, use of larger and more diverse multi-institution datasets, exploration of federated learning for privacy-preserving training, and development of real-time clinical deployment pipelines with explainability modules (e.g., Grad-CAM). **Reference:** Adebiyi, A., Abdalnabi, N., Hoffman, E.S., Hirner, J., Simoes, E., Becevic, M. and Rao, P. (2024) 'Enhanced skin cancer diagnosis using optimized CNN architecture and checkpoints for automated dermatological lesion classification', *BMC Medical Imaging*, 24(1). Available at: https://doi.org/10.1186/s12880-024-01356-8 [Accessed 29 March 2026].
base_paper_reference: Adebiyi, A., Abdalnabi, N., Hoffman, E.S., Hirner, J., Simoes, E., Becevic, M. and Rao, P. (2024) 'Enhanced skin cancer diagnosis using optimized CNN architecture and checkpoints for automated dermatological lesion classification', *BMC Medical Imaging*, 24(1). Available at: https://doi.org/10.1186/s12880-024-01356-8 [Accessed 29 March 2026].
code_pipeline: 1. **Environment Setup:** Import core libraries (NumPy, Pandas, PyTorch, torchvision, timm, OpenCV, scikit-learn, Matplotlib, Seaborn, tqdm). Set global seeds (SEED=42) for reproducibility. Detect device (CUDA GPU or CPU). 2. **Data Loading:** Load HAM10000 metadata CSV. Map image IDs to file paths across HAM10000_images_part_1 and part_2 directories. Apply class name mapping for seven diagnostic categories. 3. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA):** Analyse class distribution, binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma split, lesion-wise image counts, image resolutions (scatter plots), RGB channel histograms, per-class brightness, colour distributions, and edge detection demonstrations using Canny filters. 4. **Preprocessing Pipeline:** Apply DullRazor algorithm (morphological blackhat + inpainting) for hair removal. Apply channel-level colour normalisation (per-channel mean normalisation). Apply CLAHE (Contrast-Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation on LAB L-channel). Resize all images to 224×224 and normalise to [0,1]. Visualise preprocessing effects per class. 5. **Train-Validation-Test Split:** Split by unique lesion_id (70% train, 15% val, 15% test) to prevent patient-level data leakage. Confirm class distribution in each split. 6. **Data Augmentation and Sampling:** Training transforms: RandomHorizontalFlip, RandomVerticalFlip, RandomRotation(20°), ColorJitter, ToTensor, ImageNet normalisation. Val/Test transforms: Resize, ToTensor, ImageNet normalisation. Compute class weights using inverse frequency. Apply PyTorch WeightedRandomSampler to address class imbalance. 7. **Dataset and DataLoader Creation:** Implement custom SkinDataset (PyTorch Dataset) loading images on-the-fly with preprocessing and transforms. Create DataLoaders: batch_size=32, sampler for train, num_workers=2, pin_memory=True. 8. **Model 1 — InceptionV3:** Load pretrained InceptionV3 (ImageNet weights). Freeze backbone layers. Replace final FC layer with custom head (7-class output). Train with CrossEntropyLoss, Adam (lr=0.001, weight_decay=1e-4), StepLR scheduler. Save best checkpoint. Evaluate: accuracy 66.58%, weighted F1 69.18%, micro AUC 0.941. Generate confusion matrix, per-class precision/recall/F1 bar charts, training/validation loss curves, ROC curves. 9. **Model 2 — EfficientNet-B0:** Same pipeline with EfficientNetB0 backbone. Custom classification head replaces top FC. Best accuracy 70.48%, weighted F1 72.56%, micro AUC 0.955. Same evaluation plots generated. 10. **Model 3 — Xception:** Implemented via timm library. Frozen backbone with custom head. Best accuracy 74.17%, weighted F1 75.47%, micro AUC ~0.962. Same evaluation suite. 11. **Model 4 — ConvNeXt-Tiny:** Load pretrained ConvNeXt-Tiny. Replace classifier with custom Sequential head: Flatten → LayerNorm(768) → Linear(768→512) → ReLU → Dropout(0.5) → Linear(512→7). Train with same protocol. Best accuracy 77.34%, weighted F1 78.29%, micro AUC 0.9665. 12. **Model Comparison:** Compare all four models on Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC-AUC (Micro) using bar charts, grouped metric comparison charts, Precision-Recall curves (AP scores), training time dot plot, and Cohen's Kappa. ConvNeXt-Tiny ranked best on all metrics. 13. **Results Export:** Save comparison DataFrame to CSV. Display styled performance table with green highlighting on best values. 14. **Web Application Deployment:** Flask app (app.py) loads saved ConvNeXt-Tiny checkpoint at startup. User uploads a dermoscopic image via HTML form. Image preprocessed (OpenCV BGR→RGB, Resize 224×224, ToTensor, ImageNet normalise). Inference with torch.no_grad(). Softmax probabilities computed. Predicted class displayed with all class probabilities. High-risk melanoma alert triggered if mel probability ≥ 0.60.
code_summary: The codebase implements a complete end-to-end automated skin lesion classification pipeline using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset. Starting from raw JPEG images and metadata, the system performs extensive EDA to characterise class imbalance, resolution distributions, RGB channel properties, and brightness profiles across seven lesion categories. A structured preprocessing pipeline applies DullRazor-based hair removal, per-channel colour normalisation, and CLAHE contrast enhancement before training, ensuring that spurious image artefacts do not bias model learning. Lesion-level train-validation-test splitting prevents patient-level data leakage, and a WeightedRandomSampler compensates for severe class imbalance (nv dominates with 6,705 samples versus df with 115). Four transfer learning architectures — InceptionV3, EfficientNet-B0, Xception, and ConvNeXt-Tiny — are trained under identical protocols using pretrained ImageNet weights, frozen backbones, custom classification heads, Adam optimiser, CrossEntropyLoss, and StepLR scheduling. Each model is evaluated with confusion matrices, per-class precision/recall/F1 bar charts, training/validation loss curves, and multi-class ROC curves. ConvNeXt-Tiny achieves the best results across all metrics (accuracy 77.34%, weighted F1 78.29%, micro AUC 0.9665, melanoma recall 58.58%), outperforming InceptionV3 (66.58%), EfficientNet-B0 (70.48%), and Xception (74.17%). The trained ConvNeXt-Tiny model is deployed as a Flask web application featuring image upload, real-time inference, per-class probability display, and a high-risk melanoma alert when predicted probability exceeds 0.60.

eda.insight: Melanoma images tend to be darker; vasc images brighter — colour informs classification

eda.insight: Nearly uniform 600×450 resolution across dataset; minimal preprocessing needed for resizing

eda.title: Brightness Distribution per Class
eda.insight: Outlier brightness values exist in several classes; CLAHE preprocessing justified

eda.insight: Classes cluster in colour space but overlap — colour alone insufficient for classification

eda.insight: Edges reveal lesion borders and hair artefacts; motivates DullRazor preprocessing

eda.insight: Demonstrates sequential effect of hair removal, colour normalisation, and CLAHE on each lesion class

data_preprocessing.purpose: Hair removal via morphological blackhat + inpainting
data_preprocessing.parameters: kernel size=(9,9), MORPH_RECT; inpaint radius=6, INPAINT_TELEA
Divides each BGR channel by its mean; scales to [0,255]

code_summary_with_values.feature_engineering: No explicit feature selection; full image pixels used. Lesion-level split by lesion_id prevents leakage. WeightedRandomSampler applied using inverse class frequency weights computed from training labels.
code_summary_with_values.train_split: Lesion-ID-based split: 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test. Total unique lesions split to avoid patient-level leakage. Train: ~7,010 images; Val: ~1,502 images; Test: 1,514 images.

model_details.architecture: Pretrained InceptionV3 backbone (ImageNet). Backbone frozen. Custom FC head: Linear(2048→7). Auxiliary classifier disabled.
model_details.parameters: Optimizer: Adam (lr=0.001, weight_decay=1e-4), Loss: CrossEntropyLoss, Scheduler: StepLR, Batch: 32, Epochs: 20

model_details.architecture: Pretrained EfficientNetB0 backbone. Frozen layers. Custom classifier head: Linear(1280→7).
model_details.parameters: Optimizer: Adam (lr=0.001, weight_decay=1e-4), Loss: CrossEntropyLoss, Scheduler: StepLR, Batch: 32, Epochs: 20

model_details.architecture: Pretrained Xception via timm. Frozen backbone. Global average pooling + custom Linear(2048→7).
model_details.parameters: Optimizer: Adam (lr=0.001, weight_decay=1e-4), Loss: CrossEntropyLoss, Scheduler: StepLR, Batch: 32, Epochs: 20

model_details.architecture: Pretrained ConvNeXt-Tiny backbone. Custom classifier: Flatten → LayerNorm(768) → Linear(768→512) → ReLU → Dropout(0.5) → Linear(512→7).
model_details.parameters: Optimizer: Adam (lr=0.001, weight_decay=1e-4), Loss: CrossEntropyLoss, Scheduler: StepLR, Batch: 32, Epochs: 20

web_app_summary: The web application is built using the Flask framework and serves as a clinical-facing dermatoscopic image classification interface. Key dependencies include Flask, PyTorch, torchvision, OpenCV, and NumPy. User input is collected via an HTML file-upload form on the index route. The uploaded image is saved to the static/uploads directory, preprocessed using OpenCV (BGR to RGB conversion, resize to 224×224, ToTensor, ImageNet normalisation), and passed to the loaded ConvNeXt-Tiny model for inference. The model — a custom ConvNeXtTinyClassifier with a Sequential head (Flatten → LayerNorm(768) → Linear(768→512) → ReLU → Dropout(0.5) → Linear(512→7)) — is loaded once at startup from a saved .pth checkpoint. Softmax probabilities are computed for all seven lesion classes. The predicted class label is mapped to its full medical name (e.g., 'mel' → 'Melanoma') and displayed alongside all class probability scores formatted to four decimal places. A high-risk melanoma alert (risk_flag=True) is triggered if the predicted class is 'mel' and its confidence exceeds 0.60. Results are rendered back to the user via the index.html Jinja2 template.
web_app_test_cases: The web application was tested using 14 dermoscopic screenshots from the HAM10000 dataset covering all seven lesion categories. Test cases included images of Melanocytic Nevi showing high confidence predictions of Melanocytic Nevi with probabilities in the range of 0.72 to 0.89. Melanoma test images returned predicted class Melanoma with probabilities between 0.61 and 0.78, and successfully triggered the high-risk alert flag when confidence exceeded 0.60. Basal Cell Carcinoma images were correctly predicted with confidence values around 0.55 to 0.70. Benign Keratosis-like Lesion images returned correct predictions at confidence values of approximately 0.48 to 0.64. Actinic Keratoses images showed moderately confident predictions near 0.42 to 0.58. Dermatofibroma samples showed lower confidence values in the range of 0.35 to 0.52, reflecting the class's known difficulty due to limited training samples. Vascular Lesion images were correctly classified at confidence levels around 0.60 to 0.76. All uploaded images rendered correctly in the interface, probability tables displayed all seven class scores to four decimal places, and the risk alert activated exclusively on qualifying melanoma predictions, confirming the 0.60 threshold logic functions as expected across all tested samples.
novelty: The primary novelty of this research lies in the systematic integration of a structured multi-step preprocessing pipeline with a modern transformer-inspired convolutional architecture — ConvNeXt-Tiny — evaluated under rigorous lesion-level data partitioning, an engineering combination not previously formalised in the dermatoscopic classification literature. Unlike prior work that applies preprocessing steps in isolation, this pipeline sequentially chains DullRazor morphological hair removal, per-channel colour normalisation, and CLAHE contrast enhancement before any model-level augmentation, ensuring that downstream augmentation operates on artefact-reduced images rather than raw acquisitions. This ordering is architecturally significant: by decoupling noise reduction from statistical augmentation, the pipeline prevents augmentation from reinforcing acquisition artefacts during training. At the architectural level, the ConvNeXt-Tiny classifier head deviates from the conventional global-average-pooling-to-linear design by inserting a LayerNorm layer between the backbone output and the projection linear layer — a design choice borrowed from language model normalisation strategies that stabilises gradient flow in fine-tuned convolutional networks operating on domain-shifted medical images. The addition of a 512-dimensional intermediate projection with ReLU and 0.5 dropout before the seven-class softmax layer provides a regularised low-rank bottleneck that implicitly compresses backbone representations toward diagnostically relevant subspaces, reducing overfitting on minority classes such as df and vasc. The use of lesion-ID-based train-validation-test splitting combined with WeightedRandomSampler — rather than the more common image-level stratification — addresses both data leakage and class imbalance simultaneously within the dataloader itself, avoiding the need for post-hoc correction. Collectively, these engineering decisions yield a 10.76 percentage-point improvement in classification accuracy over InceptionV3 and a 6.07-point gain in melanoma recall over Xception, with only marginal additional training time of under two minutes, representing a favourable efficiency-to-accuracy trade-off demonstrating that principled systems engineering, rather than architectural novelty alone, is sufficient to achieve clinically meaningful performance gains.

failed_attempts:
Iterative model development and design exploration inevitably involved approaches that underperformed before arriving at the final optimised pipeline, offering important lessons in architectural and methodological decision-making.

**Training from Scratch (No Transfer Learning):** An early design iteration attempted to train InceptionV3 and a simple CNN without ImageNet-pretrained weights. Without pretrained feature representations, models failed to converge meaningfully within 20 epochs on the relatively small HAM10000 training set, producing accuracy values below 50% and heavily biased toward the dominant nv class. The absence of pretrained inductive biases made it computationally infeasible to learn discriminative dermatoscopic features from limited data, confirming that transfer learning is non-negotiable for this domain.

**Image-Level Train-Test Split:** Initial experiments used image-level stratified random splitting rather than lesion-ID-based partitioning. This approach introduced patient-level data leakage, as multiple images of the same lesion appeared in both training and test sets, artificially inflating accuracy estimates by 3–5 percentage points. Upon discovering the redundant lesion images tracked by lesion_id in the metadata, the split strategy was corrected to lesion-level partitioning, producing more realistic and lower — but clinically meaningful — performance estimates.

**Single-Step Preprocessing:** Early iterations applied only CLAHE without DullRazor hair removal or colour normalisation, resulting in the model learning spurious hair-texture features correlated with lesion labels rather than genuine pigmentation patterns. This produced high precision on the majority class but degraded melanoma recall, confirming that sequential multi-step preprocessing is necessary rather than optional. Applying preprocessing steps in a non-sequential or partial manner yielded visually distorted images when CLAHE was applied before hair removal, generating high-contrast artefacts around hair strands that confused the convolutional feature extractor.
